title: Basic characteristics of Virginia's physiographic provinces.
rows: Mostly flat; increasing minor relief in western areas
Rolling hills; steady increase in elevations east to west
High ridgeline, increase in elevation north to south
Repeating ridges and valleys. Elevations increase east to west

Table 1.3. Summary of the major river watersheds in the Commonwealth. These are the further described in the following subsection. Source: Virginia Department of Conservation and Recreation
rows: North Fork Shenandoah, South Fork Shenandoah

Levisa Fork, Russel Fork, Tug Fork

title: Generalized Climate History for the Middle Atlantic
rows: Cold and wet. Tundra-like along the ice front to the north. Spruce, fir, and sedges further south
Cold and wet. Grasslands and boreal forest. Spruce and cold climate pine dominate.
Significant warming. Spruce and fir diminish rapidly and hardwoods expand.
Development of modern climate conditions, expanding hardwood forests.
Warming and drying of climate. Oak-hickory forests dominate. Some expansion of grasslands
A general and progressive cooling trend. Significant and abrupt shifts in precipitation during mid-period.

title: Working PaleoClimate Division with Applicability to Virginia Prehistoric Research
rows: Inter-Allerod: Post Glacial: cool but w/ warming trend
Younger Dryas: sharp reversal in warming; cold, very rapid warming at end Younger Dryas
Atlantic: initial cooling with 8.2k Event, then Hypsithermal peak in warm/dry conditions
Sub-boreal; some amelioration with periodic instability; strong warming at end of period
Sub-boreal to Subatlantic: initial warm, then sharp cooling with shifts in precipitation later in period.
Subatlantic: continued moderately cool and moist, variable precipitation at times, cold episodes late
Medieval Warm Period: slight warming with generally more stable conditions, Little Ice Age
Little Ice Age: cool with severe droughts and variable precipitation
